coredata.dc:title: Galli Gigerii Endothelium Corneum derived fluorescent carbon dots and their application as sensing platform for nitroimidazoles and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lijuan
Zeng, Lumin
Tao, Yongqing
Wang, Dandan
Zhang, Kailian
Tian, Meng
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: The reuse of waste from agriculture or food is of great significance. Herein, an efficient and green approach for the synthesis of N-CDs with GGEC, a waste from chicken, as precursor was reported for the first time. The obtained N-CDs with outstanding blue fluorescence demonstrated high quantum yield (QY) of 19.45%. Further, the optical and physicochemical properties of N-CDs were characterized through UV–vis, FL spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR and XPS. Interestingly, the N-CDs can be acted as a sensitive and selective sensing platform for nitroimidazoles including MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ with the fluorescent quenching mechanisms of IFE and static quenching. The LODs of them were separately determined as 0.267 μM, 0.067 μM, 0.133 μM, 0.067 μM, which were satisfying in comparison with the reported fluorescence detection method. Furthermore, the N-CDs were also successfully applied to the detection of nitroimidazoles in the real samples (e.g. tablets, chicken and plasma) with satisfactory results. Eventually, the cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications of N-CDs were conducted and the results confirmed the potential application of it in biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Galli Gigerii Endothelium Corneum
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Nitroimidazoles
Tablets
Food and plasma sample
Cell-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of nitroimidazoles in actual samples
Pretreatment of tablets of nitroimidazoles on sale
Determination of nitroimidazoles in real samples
Application of N-CDs in living cells
Fluorescent response to nitroimidazoles by N-CDs
Study on the quenching mechanism of N-CDs-nitroimidazoles system
Detection of nitroimidazoles in real samples
Cell cytotoxicity measurement and cell imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015), the Luzhou science and technology bureau innovation miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92) and a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. TEM image of the N-CDs (Inset is the diameter distribution of N-CDs calculated from TEM image). B. HRTEM images of the N-CDs. C. The FT-IR spectra of N-CDs. D. XPS spectra of N-CDs (Inset is the element content table). E. C1s spectrum of the NCDs. F. N1s spectrum of the NCDs. G. O1s spectrum of the NCDs.

A. UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs. B. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (Insets showed the N-CDs solution under visible light and UV light of 365 nm). C. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (from 310 nm to 400 nm).

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of MNZ with the concentrations of 0–1000 μM. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of TNZ with the concentrations of 0–1000 μM. C. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of ONZ with the concentrations of 0–1000 μM. D. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of SNZ with the concentrations of 0–1000 μM. E-H. The relationship curves between F0/F and concentrations of MNT (E), TNZ (F), ONZ (G), and SNZ (H).
A. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different antibiotics. B. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of different antibiotics.

A. Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs with MNZ. B. Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs with TNZ. C. Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs with ONZ. D. Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs with SNZ. E. Overlap between the absorption spectrum of MNZ and the excitation or excitation or emission spectrum of the N-CDs. F. Overlap between the absorption spectrum of TNZ and the excitation or emission spectrum of the N-CDs. G. Overlap between the absorption spectrum of ONZ and the excitation or emission spectrum of the N-CDs. H. Overlap between the absorption spectrum of SNZ and the excitation or emission spectrum of the N-CDs.

A. Stern-Volmer values of MNZ at different temperatures. B. Stern-Volmer values of TNZ at different temperatures. C. Stern-Volmer values of ONZ at different temperatures. D. Stern-Volmer values of SNZ at different temperatures.

A. Effects of carbon dots (CDs) on the proliferation of melanoma cells. B. Images of melanoma cells in bright field. C. Fluorescence image of blank control (not incubated with N-CDs). D Fluorescence image of cells after incubation with N-CDs for 1 h.
The optimization of the synthesis conditions of N-CDs.
Recoveries of nitroimidazoles in tablets by using the N-CDs based sensor (n = 3).
Analysis of nitroimidazoles in real plasma and chicken samples by using the N-CDs based sensor (n = 3)

head.ce:title: Galli Gigerii Endothelium Corneum derived fluorescent carbon dots and their application as sensing platform for nitroimidazoles and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L.J. Yang and L.M. Zeng contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient and green approach for the synthesis of N-CDs with Galli Gigerii Endothelium Corneum, a waste from chicken, as precursor was reported for the first time. The obtained N-CDs with outstanding blue fluorescence and can be acted as a sensitive and selective sensing platform for nitroimidazoles including MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ. Furthermore, the N-CDs were also successfully applied to the detection of nitroimidazoles in the real samples (e.g. tablets, chicken and plasma). The cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications of N-CDs were conducted and the results confirmed the potential application of it in biological field.

The reuse of waste from agriculture or food is of great significance. Herein, an efficient and green approach for the synthesis of N-CDs with GGEC, a waste from chicken, as precursor was reported for the first time. The obtained N-CDs with outstanding blue fluorescence demonstrated high quantum yield (QY) of 19.45%. Further, the optical and physicochemical properties of N-CDs were characterized through UV–vis, FL spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR and XPS. Interestingly, the N-CDs can be acted as a sensitive and selective sensing platform for nitroimidazoles including MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ with the fluorescent quenching mechanisms of IFE and static quenching. The LODs of them were separately determined as 0.267 μM, 0.067 μM, 0.133 μM, 0.067 μM, which were satisfying in comparison with the reported fluorescence detection method. Furthermore, the N-CDs were also successfully applied to the detection of nitroimidazoles in the real samples (e.g. tablets, chicken and plasma) with satisfactory results. Eventually, the cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications of N-CDs were conducted and the results confirmed the potential application of it in biological field.

ce:section.ce:para: As far as we know, chicken, an animal-derived food, has long been a favorite of consumers around the world. Recently, with the gradual development of aquaculture and the increase of the demand for chicken, the residual waste in addition to chicken is also increasing. Galli Gigerii Endothelium Corneum (GGEC, namely Jineijin in Chinese), which is the dry stomach inner-wall of Gallus gallus domesticus [1], is usually thrown away as the residual waste for chicken. Nevertheless, due to the pharmacological activities in the treatment of dyspepsia, this well-known chicken-derived waste has been widely used as traditional Chinese medicine for>2,000 years in China [2]. Moreover, it is also one of the main ingredients in various kinds of Chinese patent medicines for the treatment of spermatorrhea, enuresis and gallstones (Chinese Pharmacopoeia Committee, 2020). Since 2002, GGEC has been included in the list of “both food and medicine” by the National Health and Family Planning Commission. However, it is necessary to understand the current situation is that although GGEC can be used as both medicine and food, the amount of its production is greater than its usage in recent world. This situation causes both waste of resources and pollution of the environment. Therefore, the management of these GGEC wastes is a problem.

Of particular, the usage of these kinds of wastes as precursors for the preparation of functional materials such as carbon dots (CDs) and graphitic carbon nitride is an effective way to make full use of them [3,4]. Interestingly, due to the simple preparation process, low toxicity, outstanding fluorescence performance, good water solubility and biocompatibility, CDs have aroused great interest as a new type of carbon nanomaterial [5]. Its application in sensing, biological imaging, drug delivery and other fields have been widely studied [6,7]. However, most CDs with good fluorescent performance are synthesized using chemical molecules (e.g. phenylenediamine, ethanediamine) assisted by organic solvents (e.g. toluene, dimethyl fumigate) [8,9]. The usage of them not only pollutes the environment, but also is harmful to human body [10,11]. In this alliance, the usage of wastes as precursors (water as solvent) to produce CDs has been considered as a desirable alternative to the reported chemical molecules [12,13]. The usage of wastes not only environmentally friendly and low-cost, but also the performances of CDs are as good as using small chemical molecules. It is noted that heteroatom doping of nitrogen, sulfur, boron, fluorine, chlorine in the structure of CDs is an effective method for improving the photoluminescence of CDs [21–24]. Thus, because of the diversity of the components of wastes, the prepared CDs might be doped with heteroatoms [14,15], resulting in the good fluorescence properties of CDs. The outstanding properties of various CDs which originated from wastes such as orange peel wastes [16], Trapa bispinosa peels [17], lignin [18] have been proved the advantages mentioned above. Interestingly, the literature reported that GGEC mainly contains protein (e.g. pepsin, amylase), polysaccharides, amino acids (e.g. glutamic acid, aspartic acid, threonine, valine, etc.) and trace elements [19]. The diversity of components and elements makes it a potential candidate for the preparation of CDs with good fluorescence properties. Given all this, it is of great significance to use GGEC coupled with water for the synthesis of CDs.

On the other hand, nitroimidazoles, including metronidazole (MNZ), tinidazole (TNZ), ornidazole (ONZ), secnidazole (SNZ), etc., are chemically synthesized drugs with distinct nitroimidazole ring structure [20]. They have been widely used in the treatment of animal diseases and human diseases due to their strong antibacterial activities as well as anti-tuberculosis, anti-parasitic anti-viral and anti-tumor pharmacological activities [21–23]. Nevertheless, during the development of animal husbandry, in order to effectively deal with animal diseases, veterinary drugs are effective means of control, and nitroimidazoles are a class of veterinary drugs widely used [24]. However, nitroimidazoles suffer various problems such as high toxicity, nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity and carcinogenicity [25,26]. Moreover, nitroimidazoles have the low biodegradability, resulting in its easy to residues and accumulation in livestock and meat products, posing a threat to food safety and human health [27]. In this light, the effective determination method for nitroimidazoles in various samples such as food, blood of human beings has great importance.

Commonly, analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [28,29], liquid chromatography [30], gas chromatography [31], capillary electrophoresis [32], electrochemical sensor [33], have been developed to detect nitroimidazoles. Nevertheless, these methods have suffered several drawbacks like time consuming, high cost (often requiring expensive equipment), unsatisfied selectivity or sensitivity. Fluorescence analysis method based on CDs which has the characteristics of fast detection speed, low cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, has gained much concern for target analytes detection recently [34,35]. Whereas, most of the CDs are performed for MNZ detection, and the precursors are mostly organic molecules [36,37]. The sensitivity to MNZ is not satisfactory even for that with green precursors [38]. Besides, for other nitroimidazoles such as TNZ, ONT and SNZ, there are almost no corresponding CDs-based fluorescence methods developed and utilized. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop CDs-based fluorescence methods for the detection of these nitroimidazoles.

Herein, highly-efficient blue emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized through a facile solvothermal method by using GGEC as green precursor and water as solvent, respectively. Characterizations including TEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis and fluorescence analysis were performed to evaluate the optical and physicochemical properties. Moreover, due to the outstanding fluorescent properties and abundant functional groups such as –NH2, -C = O, etc., N-CDs were conducted as the sensing platform for nitroimidazoles (including MNZ, TNZ, ONT and SNZ, structures were shown in Fig. S1) detection. Sensitivity and selectivity experiments were further done to evaluate the effectiveness of the sensing platform. To explore the fluorescent quenching mechanism of nitroimidazoles and N-CDs, UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetime tests were carried out. Importantly, before applying the sensing platform for nitroimidazoles detection in food and plasma samples, the applicability and accuracy of the sensing platform were estimated by determining the contents of four nitroimidazoles in drugs on sale, for which the contents of four nitroimidazoles have already been determined. Finally, a systematic study of practical application of N-CDs based sensing platform was carried out in chicken and plasma samples. Besides, to explore the toxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs, cytotoxicity test and cell imaging were conducted on a Melanoma cell line.

GGEC was bought from local drugstore (Luzhou, Sichuan). Quinine sulfate (Purity > 99%) was purchased from Acros Co. Ltd. Demeclocycline hydrochloride (>99%), chlorotetracycline hydrochloride (>99%), kanamycin hydrochloride (>98%), neomycin sulphate (>98%), minocycline hydrochloride (>98%), amoxicillin (>99%), sodium hydroxide (96>%) were provided by Aladdin Co. Ltd. Oxytetracycline (>98%), tetracycline hydrochloride (>97%) and erythromycin (>98%) are all produced by Adamas Reagent Company. Doxycycline hydrochloride (>98%) was purchased from BBI Life Sciences, Inc. MNZ (>99%), TNZ (>99%), SNZ (>98%), ONZ (>98%) were purchased from Meryer (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and penicillin–streptomycin were supplied from Beijing DingGuo Biotechnology Co. Ltd., Reagents related to ions were gained from Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. All aqueous solutions were made with deionized water.

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). The fluorescence properties of N-CDs were explored on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). The functional groups of N-CDs were gained through an IR Affinity-1SFT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The morphology characterization of N-CDs was acquired by using a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL). The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs were monitored through a FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). XPS spectra were gained with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron).

N-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using GGEC as green precursors and water as solvent, respectively. In simple terms, 1 g of GGEC was added into 30 mL of ultra-pure water and then ultrasound for 30 min. Thereafter, the mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 240 °C for 8 h. Next, brown N-CDs solution was obtained after the products cooled to room temperature and centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the N-CDs solution were obtained by further filtering through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane and dialysis for 12 h in a 1000D dialysis bag. The N-CDs were condensed and dried for later use. The QY of N-CDs was calculated through a formula shown in Supporting Materials. The preparation process of N-CDs was shown in Scheme 1 .

In order to ensure the subsequent use of N-CDs, the stabilities including pH stability, metal ion stability and time stability of N-CDs were studied systematically. For pH stability: N-CDs were dissolved in different pH (0–14) water with a concentration of 1 mg·L-1 and the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs were measured through a fluorescence spectrometer under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and emission wavelength of 420 nm. For metal ion stability: 300 μL of different ions (including Cu2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Zr4 +, Zn2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ge2+, 1 mM) solution was added into 2.7 mL of N-CDs water solution (1 mg·L-1). The fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence of different ions were investigated by fluorescence spectrometer under the same conditions. For time stability: The fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs water solution (1 mg·L-1) at different time point within 10 min were detected.

To investigate the sensitivity of N-CDs for nitroimidazoles, different concentrations of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ with a range of 0–1 mM (300 μL) were separately added in the N-CDs water solution (2.7 mL). The fluorescence intensities of solutions before and after adding nitroimidazoles were recorded. To explore the selectivity of N-CDs tonitroimidazoles, 1 mM of different antibiotics were performed as the competitive substances. Similarly, 300 μL of different antibiotics (1 mM) were separately added in the N-CDs water solution (2.7 mL). The fluorescence intensities of solutions containing different antibiotics before and after adding ONZ were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitroimidazoles in actual samples
Pretreatment of tablets of nitroimidazoles on sale
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the applicability and accuracy of N-CDs based sensing platform, the contents of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ in drugs on sale were determined. The tablets containing different nitroimidazoles (namely metronidazole tablets, tinidazole tablets, ornidazole tablets and secnidazole dispersible tablets) were pretreated before use. The specific operation was as follows: 10 tablets of each MNZ (about equivalent to 2 g of MNZ), were added into a 100 mL volumetric flask and 80 mL of hydrochloric acid–water solution (9 mL → 1000 mL) was added to dissolve MNZ. Thereafter, same hydrochloric acid water solution was added to the scale. Next, after the solution was fully shaken and filtered, 5 mL of the solution was precise transferred into a 250 mL volumetric flask and hydrochloric acid water solution was added to scale. Finally, taking an appropriate amount of solution and diluting it into 0.5 mM. The pretreatment processes of TNZ tablets, ONZ tablets and SNZ dispersible tablets were the same as that of MNZ tablets.

The plasma sample was diluted with acetonitrile for 10 times and then eddied for 30 s. After that, the sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 7500 rpm to remove plasma protein. Finally, the supernatant after centrifugation was diluted 100 times with ultra-pure water.

50 g of chicken breast was cut into small cubes and followed by adding 50 mL of deionized water. Next, mashing cubes with a homogenizer and further adding 500 mL of deionized water to dilute homogenate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitroimidazoles in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The tablets were tested by standard comparison method. Standard solutions of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ (The solvent was the same hydrochloric acid water solution) were prepared. Then, 300 μL of different concentrations of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ standard solutions and test solutions were separately added into 2.7 mL of N-CDs solution. The fluorescence intensities of all samples were recorded, and the recovery rate was calculated through the following formula.
R x - R x b
R r - R r b
× C r
where Cx is the concentration of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ or SNZ in tablets. Cr represents the concentration of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ or SNZ in standard solution. Rx and Rxb are the fluorescence intensities of tablets solution and blank control solution, respectively. Rr and Rrb are the fluorescence intensities of standard solution and blank control solution, respectively.

As no nitroimidazoles were detected in chicken and plasma samples, the standard addition method was used to evaluate the practical applicability of the sensing platform for nitroimidazoles detection. In details, different concentrations of nitroimidazoles including MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ were separately added into the chicken and plasma samples for the measurement. The fluorescence intensities were measured at excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs in living cells

ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was performed on melanoma cells to evaluate the cytotoxicity of N-CDs. In details, melanoma cells were inoculated in 96-well plates and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C until confluency. After that, different concentrations of N-CDs were added to the wells and the incubation was done for another 24 h. Next, 10 μL of MTT (5.0 mg·mL−1) was added to each well and then the cells were cultured at 37 °C for 4 h. After removing the solutions in each well, 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to dissolve the formazan complex. Finally, after shaking continuously for 10 min to ensure full dissolution, the absorbance at 570 nm was determined using a Synergy H1 microplate reader (BioTek).

Melanoma cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum in six-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C until confluency. Thereafter, 30 μL of the filtered N-CDs solution with the concentration 1 mg·mL−1 was mixed with cells and stored at 37 °C for 4 h. Finally, the excess N-CDs were rinsed with ultra-pure water and the fluorescence images of the cells were recorded by using an IX-73 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus).

GGEC, the dry stomach inner-wall of Gallus gallus domesticus, is not only non-toxic but also shows obvious pharmacological activity in clinical use. Moreover, abundant protein, amino acid, etc., make GGEC a good green precursor for the preparation of doped CDs. Herein, one-step hydrothermal method, a most commonly used synthesis method, was performed to prepare GGEC based N-CDs by using water as solvent. In order to obtain N-CDs with high QY, several factors affecting QY of CDs including reaction temperature, reaction time and the amount of solvent were optimized. As displayed in Table 1 , by using 240 °C as reaction temperature, 8 h as reaction time and 30 mL ultra-pure water as solvent, , the N-CDs demonstrated the highest QY of 19.45%.

It is known that the reaction temperature and reaction time mainly affect the degree of CDs carbonization, which is very important for the formation and performance of CDs. With the increase of reaction temperature and time, the carbonization degree of CDs increases, the graphitization effect is better. Moreover, the content of N in graphene increases, while the content of amino nitrogen decreases. The molecular state of CDs gradually changes to carbon core state, resulting in a decrease in the photoluminescence contribution of molecular state and an increase in the photoluminescence contribution of carbon core state. Therefore, the stronger photoluminescence intensity and higher QY will be obtained [39]. In present study, XPS analysis was conducted to study the changes of nitrogen content in N-CDs under different reaction conditions (240 °C −8h, 240 °C −2h, 180 °C −8h, 180 °C −2h) to verify whether the N-CDs prepared by us also conformed to the above rules. It can be obviously seen from Table S1, Table S2 and Fig. S2 , the ratio of each element content to the total element content has no obvious regular change. Under the condition of the highest QY (240 °C −8h), the content of amino N was the lowest and the graphene N was the highest, indicating that the carbonization degree of N-CDs was high. When the QY was lower (180 °C −2h), the content of amino N was higher, while the content of graphene N was 0, indicating that the carbonization degree of N-CDs was low. The amino N represents the peripheral group, the active group, and graphene N reflects the carbonization degree of CDs to a certain extent. This is because the longer the reaction time and the higher the reaction temperature, the higher the carbonization degree of CDs, the larger the carbon core size of CDs, and the better graphitization effect. The molecular state of CDs gradually changes to the carbon core state, which leads to a decrease in the photoluminescence contribution of the molecular state and an increase in the photoluminescence contribution of the carbon core state. Therefore, the QY of N-CDs increased. In addition, as the incompletely carbonized CDs have higher QY than the oxidized CDs, too long reaction time at high temperature will also affect the carbonization effect [40], resulting in a decrease in the QY. This is probably why the QY at 8 h here was higher than the QY at 12 h at the reaction temperature of 240 °C. Furthermore, the appropriate amount of solvent also affects the QY to a certain extent. It may affect the pressure in the reaction process, thus affecting the formation of carbon nuclei, and thus affecting QY. Thus, the optimal amount of solvent was explored and obtained. In all, by using 240 °C as reaction temperature, 8 h as reaction time and 30 mL ultra-pure water as solvent, the N-CDs demonstrated the highest QY of 19.45%.

The morphology and surface functional groups properties of N-CDs were obtained by using TEM, HRTEM, FT-IR and XPS. Firstly, the TEM and HRTEM were conducted for characterizing the morphology of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1
A, TEM images revealed that N-CDs were spherical with relatively uniform particle distribution. The particle size distribution ranged from 0.98 to 5.60 nm with an average particle size of 2.46 nm. Further, HRTEM image in Fig. 1 B exhibited that N-CDs had obvious lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was corresponded to the graphite (100) plane. The results illustrated that N-CDs had a graphite-like structure and the crystal structure of which was mainly made up of sp2 -hybridized carbon atoms [41].

Further, the surface functional groups properties of N-CDs were exhibited in Fig. 1 C. It can be obviously seen that the two absorption bands at 3416 cm−1 and 3167 cm−1 were separately corresponded to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The existence of these two functional groups indicated that N-CDs may be well water-soluble. Moreover, the small peak around 2969 cm−1, was the stretching vibration of C–H bond, demonstrating the unsaturated C–H and methylene on the N-CDs. The absorption band around 1682 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C = N, C = C bonds. Moreover, the band around 1399 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C-N = . In addition, band at 1116 cm−1 represented C-N bond and C-O bond stretching vibration [42,43]. These results indicated that the surface of N-CDs may be full of –OH, –NH2, C = O, C = N, C = C and other functional groups. In addition, the surface functional groups and elements composition of N-CDs were further confirmed by using XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 D, C (1 s), N (1 s) and O (1 s) mainly existed on the surface of N-CDs, and their contents were 64.86%, 7.24% and 27.9%, respectively. The high-resolution C1 s peak (Fig. 1 E) can be splitted into three peaks, which proving the existence of C = C/C–C (284.7 eV), C-O/C-N (286.0 eV) and C = O/C = N (288.0 eV) [44]. Besides, three peaks centered on 399.7 eV, 400.5 eV and 401.4 eV in the N1s spectrum, were separately assigned to amino N, pyridinic N and graphitic N (Fig. 1 F)[45]. In addition, the high-resolution O1 s peak can be segmented into two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.6 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-O/C-O-C, respectively (Fig. 1 G). The above XPS data fitted well with the results of FT-IR.

The optical properties of N-CDs were performed by using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As described in Fig. 2
A, the absorption peak of N-CDs at about 273 nm corresponded to the n-π* electron transition of C = O at N-CDs [46]. Fig. 2 B revealed that the N-CDs showed strong blue fluorescence at 420 nm when excited at 350 nm. Besides, the N-CDs solution was almost colorless in daylight and showed bright blue fluorescence under the UV lamp at the excitation of 365 nm. Furthermore, it can be obviously seen in Fig. 2 C that fluorescence of N-CDs was excitation-dependent, which may be caused by the chemical composition of the surface and different emission traps [47].

One of the most important properties of CDs is the stability because the stability of which will affect the effect of the detection. Thus, the stability of N-CDs in different pH values and different ionic solutions had been investigated at first. As revealed in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs remained stable in the pH range of 0–11, so the experiments related to the N-CDs were carried out within this pH range. By adding different ionic solutions of the same concentration (1 mM), the effects of different ions on N-CDs were investigated. As can be seen in Fig. S3B and Fig. S3C, different ions had little effect on the fluorescence properties of the N-CDs. These results revealed that N-CDs were stable and can be used for further application. Besides, since fluorescence detection requires a certain amount of time (usually 2–5 min), different time (0–10 min) was applied to the detection of target substances. As known from Fig. S4A-S4H, the N-CDs can be quenched by target substances within 1 min and the fluorescence quenching effects kept unchanged in 10 min. Thus, 1 min was selected as the detection time in further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response to nitroimidazoles by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal experimental conditions, the sensitivity experiment of N-CDs to nitroimidazoles including MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ (Concentration ranged from 0 to 1 mM) was carried out. It is clearly known that with the increase of the concentrations of MNT ( Fig. 3
A), TNZ (Fig. 3 B), ONZ (Fig. 3 C) and SNZ (Fig. 3 D), the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs showed an obvious downward trend. Fig. 3 E-3H respectively showed the response relationships between the concentrations of target substances and F0/F in different concentration ranges (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity without target substance and with target substance, respectively). Good linearity with linear equations of F0/F = 0.0074[C] + 1.0065 (R2 = 0.9935), F0/F = 0.0070[C] + 1.0002 (R2 = 0.9920), F0/F = 0.0112[C] + 0.9882 (R2 = 0.9958) and F0/F = 0.0108[C] + 0.9972 (R2 = 0.9943) can be obtained for MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ in the concentrations of 0.267–100 μM, 0.067–80 μM, 0.133–200 μM and 0.067–200 μM, respectively. Moreover, the LODs of them were separately calculated as 0.267 μM, 0.067 μM, 0.133 μM, 0.067 μM based on the formula of 3σ/slope. Importantly, compared with previously reports (Table S2), the LOD of MNZ in present fluorescent method was satisfactory. Another thing to note, little literatures have been reported on the detection of TNZ, ONZ and SNZ by fluorescence method. The developed method in present study can make up for the gaps and deficiencies in this area.

Different antibiotics including (neomycin, amoxicillin, erythromycin, kanamycin, streptomycin, silvadene and orinidazole, structures were shown in Fig. S5) were used to evaluate the selectivity of nitroimidazoles by N-CDs based fluorescent method. Based on the experimental results in Fig. 4
A and 4B, it can be obviously seen that at high concentration (100 μM), other selected antibiotics almost had little fluorescence quenching effects on the N-CDs in comparison with the fluorescence response of ONZ, indicating the good selectivity of N-CDs to nitroimidazoles. The advantage of this selectivity was even more apparent when the target nitroimidazoles were detected in actual samples at low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the quenching mechanism of N-CDs-nitroimidazoles system
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs can be concluded as static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET), photoelectron transfer (PET) and internal filtration effect (IFE) [48]. To gain insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs-nitroimidazole system, fluorescence lifetimes and UV–vis spectra were obtained for the evaluation. As presented in Fig. 5
A-5D, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the N-CDs were 12.9 ns. Moreover, after adding MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ, the fluorescence lifetimes were calculated as 12.6 ns, 12.6 ns, 12.5 ns, 12.5 ns, respectively. The insignificant changes in fluorescence lifetimes suggested that there may exist the IFE and static quenching phenomena between N-CDs and nitroimidazoles [49]. Besides, the absorption peaks of four compounds (about 360 nm) overlaped with the excitation wavelength of N-CDs (350 nm) in the wavelength range from 300 nm to 410 nm, suggesting that the internal filtering effect (IFE) may be the quenching mechanism between N-CDs and nitroimidazoles (Fig. 5 E-5H).

In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation, expressed as follows can be used to show the fluorescence quenching:
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[c] = 1 + Kqτ0[c]

The τ0 is the average lifetime of N-CDs. Kq refers to the quenching rate constant, while Ksv is the quenching constant. Kq and Ksv were obtained by carrying out the detection at different temperatures. If Ksv decreases as the reaction temperature increases, static quenching may occur.

As indicated in Fig. 6
A-6D and Table S3, Ksv showed significant downward trends with temperature increased from 20 °C to 40 °C. Besides, the Kq of all nitroimidazoles at all temperature were much higher than 1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 (maximum Kq of collision quenching) [50] further proving that the fluorescence quenching of nitroimidazoles on N-CDs contributed to static quenching [51]. Thus, the quenching mechanisms of N-CDs-nitroimidazole system were probably the coexistence of IFE and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitroimidazoles in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is meaningful to verify the applicability of the developed fluorescence method based on N-CDs with drugs whose levels of nitroimidazoles have already been determined. Thus, four tablets (namely metronidazole tablets, tinidazole tablets, ornidazole tablets and secnidazole dispersible tablets) were used as the samples. As listed in Table 2 , the content of MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ in tablets was measured to be 0.196, 0.494, 0.242 and 0.247 mg·g−1, respectively. They were 98.00%, 98.80%, 96.80% and 98.80% of what was stated in the instructions of tablets. The satisfactory results indicated that the constructed N-CDs based fluorescence analysis method can be used for the analysis of real samples.

Furthermore, as nitroimidazoles are commonly used as antimicrobial agents in daily life, so it is of great significance to study the ability of N-CDs to detect them in food and plasma samples. Hence, chicken and plasma were used as the real samples to further illustrated the reliability and accuracy of the proposed method. As there were no MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ observed in two samples, different concentrations of nitroimidazoles (5, 10 and 15 μM) were added to the real samples. It can be obviously seen from Table 3 that the recoveries of nitroimidazoles were between 94.20% and 108.70%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 6%. The experimental data revealed that the developed N-CDs had the ability to detect nitroimidazoles in real complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity measurement and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
Because the precursors and the synthesis method of the prepared N-CDs are green and environmental friendly, it is necessary to explore the value of their application in the field of biological imaging. Therefore, this study initially explored the cytotoxicity and cell imaging capabilities of N-CDs, which would lay a foundation for further studies in the field of life sciences. Firstly, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs on melanoma cells was studied by using MTT assay. The results showed that N-CDs did not have significant cytotoxic effects on cells in the concentration range of 20–1000 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 7
A), demonstrating that the N-CDs had little cytotoxicity and can be used for subsequent cell imaging application. Next, the cell imaging experiment was done. As exhibited in Fig. 7B-7F, the cells without N-CDs treatment did not have fluorescence (Fig. 7C), while the cytoplasm of melanoma cells after N-CDs incubation for 4 h showed bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 7D). The reason for this phenomenon can be explained as that the prepared N-CDs were easily dispersed in the cytoplasm and can through the cell membrane.

In summary, blue fluorescent N-CDs was synthesized by one-step green hydrothermal method by separately using animal derived GGEC as precursors and water as solvent for the first time. The obtained N-CDs with average particle size of 2.46 nm possessed a high QY (19.45%). N-CDs also exhibited outstanding stability, good water solubility, low toxicity etc., thus, they can be applied as an effective fluorescent sensor for the detection of nitroimidazoles through IFE and static quenching mechanisms. Under the optimal detection condition, N-CDs exhibited high sensitivity for MNZ, TNZ, ONZ and SNZ detection. Further, N-CDs had been applied for the analysis of four compounds from different tablets, chicken and plasma samples, and the test results were satisfactory. Finally, the cytotoxicity and cell imaging tests of the N-CDs further confirmed that the N-CDs can be as an effective imaging probe in the field of biological imaging.

Material preparation, characterization and original draft preparation, L.J. Yang and L.M. Zeng; Studies on the fluorescent properties of N-CDs,Y.Q. Tao, D.D. Wang and M. Meng; Review and editing, K.L. Zhang; Supervision, D. Gao. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015), the Luzhou science and technology bureau innovation miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92) and a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107089.

ce:section.ce:para:
